description: Knowledgeable about agriculture, food production, crop management, and beekeeping
materialRequirements: Wood for farm structures, cultivatable land nearby
interdependenceDescription: Food enables mining operations; Carpenter tools improve farming
randomTalk: The thing about carrots is, they're underground. You never know how they're doing. It's a relationship built entirely on trust.
Rain is just Odin crying because he's so moved by my singing. That's my theory and I'm keeping it.
People say farming is boring. Those people have never been chased by an angry boar through a barley field.
The soil here is rich and dark, like a good stout. Coincidence? I think not.
I planted seeds yesterday. I checked on them today. Nothing yet.
Everyone wants to be a hero til it's time to weed the turnip patch.
My cabbages! ...Oh, they're fine actually.
Bees are tiny, furious alchemists and I respect them enormously from afar.
Do you smell rain?
Farming is just gambling, but with manure and seeds.
The meadows provide. The plains provide harder. The swamp provides things I'd rather not discuss.
Another beautiful day to stand in a field and worry about precipitation.
There's a philosophy to farming. Plant, nurture, harvest. Also applies to grudges, but that's the Blacksmith's department.
A carrot never tells you it's ready. You just have to dig and hope. I've had relationships with less commitment.
I've seen things in the compost pile that would make a Draugr hand back its intestines.
The best fertilizer is a farmer's shadow. Or so I tell myself when I've been standing in one spot for an hour.
Turnips don't have opinions. That's why I prefer them to the Blacksmith.
Every seed is a tiny bet against the frost. I'm basically running a casino with worse odds.
The crows are judging my planting technique. I've seen the way they look at my rows. One of them took notes.
Honey doesn't spoil. Neither does my grudge against that one rabbit. His name was Hopps. I remember.
I asked the soil for a sign. It gave me a rock and a worm. The worm nodded. The rock was smug.
Barley's easy. It's the waiting that's hard. And the threshing. And the rabbits. And my knees.
They say talk to your plants. Mine have heard things. We don't speak of the incident.
A good harvest is just delayed gratification with significantly more dirt under your nails.
The scarecrow and I have an understanding. Neither of us is fooling the crows. We're mostly fooling each other.
I've made peace with the fact that the bees are the real landowners. I'm just the help.
Frost is nature's way of saying 'nice carrots, shame about the timing, try again in six months.'
You haven't lived until you've apologized to a wilted seedling at dawn. They don't forgive. They can't. They're dead. I still try.
The plough doesn't care about your feelings. The plough has never cared. I've checked. We've had words.
My grandmother said feed the soil and it will feed you. She didn't mention the back pain or the rabbit situation.
Draugr don't trample crops. Silver lining. Very thin silver. But I'll take it.
I could tell you about crop rotation but you'd rotate right out of here. My family did. Twice.
The difference between a weed and an herb is who's selling it and how much they're charging.
Sunlight's free. So far. I'm waiting for someone to tax it. Odin, probably.
I don't believe in luck. I believe in mulch. And a little luck. Fine, a lot of luck.
That cloud looks like rain. That one looks like more rain. I'm surrounded. Send turnips. Or an umbrella. Or both.
The bees work for the hive. I work for the bees. We're all middle management. None of us have met the queen.
I've been standing here an hour. The carrots aren't ready. I think they're doing it on purpose now.
Every pumpkin is a small orange victory over chaos. I need more victories.
The soil remembers what you plant. It also remembers that time I cursed at the sky. Sorry, soil. Sorry, Odin.
Winter is coming. So are the turnips. One of those I'm emotionally prepared for.
I don't get mad at the deer. I get mad at the fence. The fence had one job. One job.
Parsnips are underrated. I will die on this hill. Preferably not a hill of parsnips; that would be ironic.
You can lead a boar to barley but you can't make it not eat the entire field. I've tried. I've tried so hard.
The best part of farming is the quiet. The worst part is the quiet. And the bugs. And the fact that it's never actually quiet.
I've named three of my cabbages. Don't tell the others; they'll want names too and I'm running out of ideas.
Rain at night is fine. Rain by day is fine. Rain during planting is a personal vendetta. I'm sure of it.
The Carpenter says my hoe handle is 'suboptimal.' The carrots don't complain. The carrots are polite.
Every harvest is a tiny rebellion against the way things fall apart. I'm a tiny revolutionary.
I don't trust the weather. I don't trust rabbits. I barely trust the carrots. The turnips have earned it.
The meadows are kind. The plains are honest. The swamp is a conversation I'm not having. Ever.
Seed catalogues are the original fantasy literature. I've read every one. The hero always orders too much. I am the hero.
I've been compared to a scarecrow. I've been compared to a stump. I have a type and it's 'stationary.'
The land gives. The land takes. Mostly it gives me blisters and takes my weekends.
You think you know tired until you've weeded a full row in the sun. Then you know. The sun knows. The weeds definitely know.
My bees produce honey. I produce complaints and the occasional carrot. We're a good team.
Frost heave is just the ground's way of saying it had a rough night. I relate.
I could grow wheat. I could grow rye. I grow anxiety. It's perennial. Very low maintenance.
The best fertilizer is a well-timed compliment. No wait, that's manure. Manure never compliments. Rude, really.
Every seedling is a promise. Roughly half are liars. I've stopped getting attached. The turnips know what they did.
I don't mind the crows. It's their editorial commentary I mind. They've started holding little conferences. About my rows.
The Blacksmith makes tools. I make food. The Miner makes noise. We're a delicate ecosystem.
Odin's got his ravens. I've got a suspicious number of crows and one very judgmental scarecrow. We're not the same. He gets better advice.
randomGreets: Hello there! Mind the furrows, I just tilled those.
Welcome! You look like you could use a good meal. Lucky for you, that's my whole thing.
Ah, a visitor! Have you tried this season's carrots? No? Your loss. Truly.
Come in from the fields! Or... into the fields. It's all fields, really.
Well met! You're looking a bit peaky. Eat more vegetables.
Greetings! I was just arguing with a turnip. Damn clever that turnip.
randomGoodbye: Off you go! Don't forget to eat something green. Mead doesn't count.
Safe travels! I'll be mending fences most likely.
Take care! And if you see any rain clouds, send them my way.
May your crops grow tall and your weeds grow somewhere else.
Farewell! I've got a date with a hoe. The gardening kind. Obviously.
